A tour of the web technology I use to make this site as fast as possible.

import ArticleImage from '../components/ArticleImage.astro';
import AF from '/src/images/astroflamethrower.png';

Here's a list of things your computer can do in under 16.67ms : 
- [Procedurally generate this mine cart animation](https://www.shadertoy.com/view/Ms2fWW)
- [Run a 100 node cloth simulation](https://www.shadertoy.com/view/4dG3R1)
- [Iterate over 400MB of RAM](https://www.transcend-info.com/Support/FAQ-292)
- [Guess 4.8 Billion Password Hashes (if you have an RTX 4090)](https://www.tomshardware.com/news/eight-rtx-4090s-can-break-passwords-in-under-an-hour)
- [Simulate every particle in a game of Noita](https://thumbs.gfycat.com/AbandonedDownrightAmurratsnake-size_restricted.gif)

In the year 2022 theres no reason why a simple webpage should take much longer than that to load on good
hardware with a good connection.

The technologies I use in this website to achieve that are :  
spitting.alpacacdn.com
<ArticleImage src={AF} width='400px' alt="flamethrower and astro logos"></ArticleImage>

1. Astro - a super lightweight framework that prerenders everything at build time and boasts intercompatibility with every other major framework.
2. Flamethrower - a client side router that pre fetches visible links and intercepts page transitions to convert a multi page app to a single page one. 

That's all, just 2 pieces of software for my front end. Astro can also handle api endpoints so I could 
deploy on Netlify and use SSR and be done. The flexibility afforded by that simplicity has advantages too - 

- I could write a simple server with Axum.rs, integrate Postgres into it with Diesel, and deploy the whole
thing on Linode as a static site, if I needed to make a website like Wikipedia or the Internet Archive,
which just need to serve a huge amount of static pages efficiently. 
- I could target a microprocessor and write a simple static file server in C using the Arduino libraries
and just serve up the compiled build folder, which, minus the images, is only 11KB.
With the (unoptimized) images its 856KB. That's small enough to be viable for even embedded devices 
like the esp32, which have wifi compatibility but less than 4MB of onboard storage. 

## When To Consider Flamethrower + Astro

The argument could be made for many websites that it doesn't make economic sense for them to make their 
service so responsive - prefetching the amount of links present in a Wikipedia article would blow up
their server bandwidth dozens of times over.

Fortunately though Flamethrower offers you the option to only prefetch links when the user starts hovering
over them - the body swap done in js is much faster and more responsive than an entire page load, and it
preserves state between pages. 

## Setup Instructions

The project setup using this stack with rust is as follows (skip to step 5 if you don't care about Rust): 

1. make a git repo with rust gitignore
2. open with vs code
3. initialize new rust project by typing the following in the terminal : cargo new 
4. copy paste the cargo.toml below 
5. make a new folder in project root beside src, make astro project following instructions on their [github](https://docs.astro.build/en/getting-started/)
6. if you want to use svelte : npx astro add svelte
7. npm i flamethrower-router
8. go to layout.astro and add the import lines to the layout.astro file's head.
```javascript
    <script>
        import flamethrower from 'flamethrower-router';
        flamethrower({ prefetch: 'visible', log: true, pageTransitions: false });
    </script>
```

For an example file based router if you're going the pure static site generator route with astro, look at [this example](https://github.com/tokio-rs/axum/blob/main/examples/static-file-server/src/main.rs)

Cargo.toml 
```toml
[package]
name = "axum-sandbox"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

# See more keys and their definitions at https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/manifest.html

[dependencies]
axum = "0.5.16"
tokio = {version="1.0", features=["full"]}
serde = "1.0.145"
axum-extra = {version="0.3.7" , features=["spa"]}
tower-http = {version="0.3.0" , features=["fs" , "trace"]}
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = {version="0.3", features=["env-filter"]}
```

And voila, you've got a static site that should be as fast as it possibly can be. You don't have dynamic routes with this, unless you can figure out
how to make Astro generate a page from Rust.

# Hello!

## This is the first post
Solving the hardest Rust problem on Code Wars

It was uploaded in 2017 and I was the 13th person in the world solve it in Rust, and 212th for all languages, though around 9400 people have attempted it.  
The problem is a brutal version of a problem from Machine Learning called N-Queens.  
In the N queens problem your goal is to, given a chess board of dimension N (a normal board is 8x8 so N=8), arrange N queens on the board such that none are
attacking eachother.

This challenge was difficult for 3 reasons - 
- The typical DFS implementation of the solution was only viable for board sizes up to around 28-30, before it just took too much time to complete.
- The board sizes this problem tests on are up to 1000x1000 (computational difficulty scales exponentially for DFS).
- Not only does the problem require around 10 different solutions to different boards in the 10 second time limit, the boards can also have no solution.

I was able to solve it due to 4 key innovations
- I created a genetic algorithm that maintains a population of 'boards' that reproduce based on their fitness - aka the amount of queens under attack.
- The boards prioritize swapping queens that are under attack, and a list of pairs of attacking queens is created optimally while the board is generated.
- The boards were able to be cloned, mutated, and evaulated extremely efficiently, doing so in a single iteration and without any memory allocations.
- I realized all unsolvable boards followed a pattern, and automatically failed boards that matched it. 

From there, I just optimized the population size and threw rayon at it, and soon enough I could solve those 1000x1000 boards in milliseconds. The code for my solution is available [here](https://github.com/Daniel-A-Gutierrez/rust_sandbox/blob/master/src/experiments/refactored_nqueens.rs).
Quick And Easy KV Store With SQLite
Implementing a HTTP KV Store with SQLite in rust in 200 lines.

import CodeSnippet from '/src/components/CodeSnippet.astro'; # Meta Introductory level tutorials and articles often glaze over errors and the struggle involved in producing the code therein, but learning to interpret the errors the compiler gives you is the hardest and most important skill in learning a language, so to that end, I went out of my way to show as many common mistakes as possible, as well as including the multitide of genuine mistakes the compiler helped me catch as I wrote the code for this tutorial. # What Makes SQLite Special? SQLite is the most widely used database in the world - searching for it in the applications you use on a daily basis is a bit like turning over a rock in the woods looking for bugs. It sits at the core of the IOS filesystem, Web Browsers, and is available as an official plugin for the Unreal Engine. SQLite is unique from most other databases in that it is in-processes, acting more like a library rather than a distinct application or binary running on the client machine. SQLite databases are also constrained to a single file and thread, making them simple to manage and backup, while retaining many of the features and familiarity of SQL. The reason I love it though, is that its the quickest and easiest way to prototype a database application. Here's how you can get started in rust. # Project Setup This tutorial makes no assumptions about your programming knowledge, I've written it assuming the reader knows nothing about rust and intends to follow along on their own. If you don't have rust installed, [install it](https://rust-lang.org/tools/install/). Follow any additional instructions the installer gives you, then navigate to a directory where you want to create a new project.

cargo new sqlite_example

Open the new folder in vscode, and install the rust-analyzer extension, Even better toml, and CodeLLDB (optional). The new folder will contain a ```cargo.toml``` file, paste the following under the [dependencies] section. ```toml ... [dependencies] anyhow = { version = "1.0.71" , features = ["std", "backtrace"]} axum = { version = "0.6.18", features = ["macros"] } axum-extra = { version = "0.7.4", features = ["cookie", "form", "multipart", "cookie-private"] } axum-server = "0.5.1" hyper = { version = "0.14.26", features = ["http2"] } tokio = { version = "1.28.2", features = ["full"] } rusqlite = { version = "0.37.0", features = ["bundled"] } ``` For a quick explanation of our dependencies: - anyhow is an error handling library that allows for dynamic error types. - hyper is an http server. - axum is a router that wraps hyper. It maps incoming http requests to handler functions, and their outputs to http responses. - Tokio is rust's async runtime which hyper and axum depend on. - rusqlite is our sqlite library. Copy this into main.rs. ```rust use std::net::SocketAddr; use axum::{self,routing::{get,post,put,delete}}; use tokio; use anyhow::{Result, ensure}; use rusqlite::{Connection, self, OptionalExtension}; #[tokio::main] //a macro that tells tokio this is our entrypoint. async fn main() { let addr = SocketAddr::from(([127,0,0,1], 3334));//0.0.0.0 for outfacing let api_router = axum::Router::new() .route("/hello", get( || async {"hello!"}) ) //our /hello handler is the closure we give to get .into_make_service(); let _server = hyper::Server::bind(&addr).serve(api_router).await.unwrap(); } ``` Now we should be able to run ```cargo run``` and start up our server! I'm on Linux so I'll just use curl to test it, but if you're on Windows or MacOS use the Thunderclient extension of vscode or postman.

curl localhost:3334/hello

should return

hello!

Now we're ready to get on to the sql. # Initializing the Database To start with, we just make a connection to a new in-memory database. Our table is going to have 4 columns - id, unixtime, key, value. ```rust async fn init_db() -> Result { let conn = Connection::open_in_memory()?; //define schema return Ok(conn); } ``` This defines an async function which returns a future (implicitly because its async) that yields a Result\ - either Ok\ or Err\. In rust async functions must be awaited on to run, unlike javascript which automatically runs the future and only pauses execution of the current task when it is awaiting another. Running a task asynchronously from the current one requires a call to tokio::spawn(). Connection::open_in_memory also returns a result. The question mark operator signifies that should that be an error, return it. ```rust async fn init_db() -> Result { let conn = Connection::open_in_memory()?; //define schema conn.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS Storage (id : INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, unixtime: INTEGER, key : UNIQUE TEXT, value : TEXT); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_storage_keys ON storage (key); ", ())?; return Ok(conn); } ``` Here we make the table, and create an index on our key column so it can function efficiently as a KV store. Our time is an integer because we're going to use the builtin unixepoch() function, which returns the number of seconds since Jan 1 1970. # Inserting Into Our KV Store Lets first make a function that inserts a key value pair into our database. It should check and return different errors in the condition that 1. the key or value are too big, 2. the key is already present, 3. something else went wrong. It doesn't actually need to be async because rusqlite's in-memory database is fully synchronous. Lastly, it needs to take its arguments by reference to avoid unnecessarily copying or consuming them. ```rust enum SetError { Oversized, KeyAlreadyPresent, DBError(rusqlite::Error) } async fn db_set(conn : &Connection, key : &str, val : &str) -> Result<() , SetError> { if key.len() > 10_000 || val.len() > 10_000 { return Err(SetError::Oversized) }; todo!(); } ``` A decent start! Also note the todo!() macro - this will cause a panic if it runs but silences errors relating to the incompleteness of the function otherwise. Lets continue with ```conn.execute("INSERT INTO storage (unixepoch(),$1,$2);", (key, val))?```. Key takes the place of $1, and val takes $2. However, we have an error :

`?` couldn't convert the error to `SetError`

Theres 2 ways to solve this - we either implement the From trait for SetError to allow it to automatically be converted by ? from a rusqlite error, or we map the error type. We'll do the latter since its only used this one time. ```rust fn db_set(conn : &Connection, key : &str, val : &str) -> Result<() , SetError> { if key.len() > 10_000 || val.len() > 10_000 { return Err(SetError::Oversized) }; conn.execute("INSERT INTO storage (unixepoch(),$1,$2);", (key, val)) .map_err( |e| { match e.sqlite_error_code() { Some(rusqlite::ErrorCode::ConstraintViolation) => SetError::KeyAlreadyPresent, _ => SetError::DBError(e), } })?; return Ok(()); } ``` Map Error takes a closure - an inline function with syntax that looks like |args| \{implicit return value} - that expects to be given the error and returns a different kind of error. In our case, we are doing a match on the value of e.sqlite_error_code(). I don't know why this yields an option but regardless that means we have to wrap the error type we're looking for in Some(T) since an Option's variants are Some(T) and None. The T we're looking for is rusqlite::ErrorCode::ConstraintViolation, and with the fat arrow we map it to our SetError enum. Anything other than that (signified with '_') we just wrap in SetError:DBError(e). Constraint Violation is technically any constraint but our table's only constraint is the uniqueness one, so its fair to assume that it means the key is already present. And thats about it for inserting into our KV Store! We cant test it till we make a way to retrieve values so lets do that next. # Reading The KV Store First step - read some documentation. I found this function in the rusqlite crate that seems to do what we want, lets break it down. ``` rust pub fn query_one(&self, sql: &str, params: P, f: F) -> Result where P: Params, F: FnOnce(&Row<'_>) -> Result, ```
"Convenience method to execute a query that is expected to return exactly one row. Returns Err(QueryReturnedMoreThanOneRow) if the query returns more than one row. Returns Err(QueryReturnedNoRows) if no results are returned. If the query truly is optional, you can call .optional() on the result of this to get a Result\> (requires that the trait rusqlite::OptionalExtension is imported)."
It takes 3 generic type parameters. - P is the simplest, its just a tuple of things that can be parameters to a prepared statement, just like the execute function we used earlier. - T is the desired output of this function. We should make a KVRow struct to map our database storage table into rust. - F is a function or closure that takes in a &Row, and attempts to convert it to T, returning a result. Lets start out with something like this. ``` rust fn db_get(conn: Connection, key: &str) -> Result> { // if the key is too big it cant be in the database if key.len() > 10_000 { return Ok(None); } conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", {key}, |row| {...} ); todo!(); } ``` We end up with a couple errors, in part because F isn't really defined but also this one. ``` example.rs(69, 70): this tail expression is of type `&str` example.rs(69, 10): required by a bound introduced by this call lib.rs(684, 12): required by a bound in `rusqlite::Connection::query_one` example.rs(69, 69): use a unary tuple instead: `(`, `,)` ``` Still in the habit of writing jsx I guess, \{key} should be written as (key,), since it's supposed to be a tuple. As for implementing F, I think the following trait called TryFrom will be useful. ``` rust struct GreaterThanZero(i32); impl TryFrom for GreaterThanZero { type Error = &'static str; fn try_from(value: i32) -> Result { if value <= 0 { Err("GreaterThanZero only accepts values greater than zero!") } else { Ok(GreaterThanZero(value)) } } } ``` In rust, structs are data, and traits are groups of functions associated with a struct. You implement a trait for a struct by defining the trait's members. The TryFrom\ trait has a type parameter T (what we're converting from) and 2 members : Error and try_from(value: T) -> etc. . We can use try_from to go from a sqlite database row to some defined type in a clean idiomatic way. Here's my first crack at it : ``` rust #[derive(Debug)] struct KVRow { id : i64, unixtime: i64, key : String, value: String, } impl TryFrom for KVRow { type Error = anyhow::Error; fn try_from(row: Row) -> std::result::Result { let id = row.get(0)?; let unixtime = row.get(1)?; let key = row.get(2)?; let value = row.get(3)?; return KVRow{id,unixtime,key,value}; } } fn db_get(conn: Connection, key: &str) -> Result> { // if the key is too big it cant be in the database if key.len() > 10_000 { return Ok(None); } conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", {key}, |row| ); todo!(); } ``` And of course, errors. Lets see what the compiler has to say. ``` implicit elided lifetime not allowed here expected lifetime parameterrustcClick for full compiler diagnostic example.rs(52, 17): indicate the anonymous lifetime: `<'_>` ``` So apparently Row has some internal reference to the database or connection it came from, and thus, a lifetime. Rust uses lifetimes to track referential validity - a struct that stores references can only live as long as what it references. We don't need to know the details or use it in our case, we just have to acknowledge that and specify Row as Row\<'_>, where '_ is the explicitly elided lifetime. ``` mismatched types expected enum `Result` found struct `KVRow` ``` Ooops, a simple mistake. try_from's last line should be ``` rust // return KVRow{id,unixtime,key,value}; return Ok( KVRow{id,unixtime,key,value} ) ; ``` After correcting those and updating F to use try_from(row) ... ``` rust fn db_get(conn: Connection, key: &str) -> Result> { // if the key is too big it cant be in the database if key.len() > 10_000 { return Ok(None); } conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| KVRow::try_from(row)); todo!(); } ``` Hooray, new compiler errors, its like its my birthday or something! ``` mismatched types expected enum `Result<_, rusqlite::Error>` found enum `Result`rustcClick for full compiler diagnostic example.rs(69, 83): try wrapping the expression in `Ok`: `Ok(`, `)` > the trait bound `KVRow: From<&Row<'_>>` is not satisfied the trait `TryFrom<&Row<'_>>` is not implemented for `KVRow` but trait `TryFrom>` is implemented for it for that trait implementation, expected `Row<'_>`, found `&Row<'_>` required for `&Row<'_>` to implement `Into` required for `KVRow` to implement `TryFrom<&Row<'_>>`rustcClick for full compiler diagnostic ``` Theres a lot of junk here but the important thing is line 5 which i marked with a '>'. Indeed, we implemented TryFrom for Row, not &Row. We could simply call row.clone() and duplicate it then destroy it to make our KVRow, or we could alter the trait impl to take a &Row instead and actually make the whole thing cheaper. Lets do that. ``` rust //change Row to &Row here impl TryFrom<&Row<'_>> for KVRow { type Error = anyhow::Error; //and here. fn try_from(row: &Row) -> std::result::Result { let id = row.get(0)?; let unixtime = row.get(1)?; let key = row.get(2)?; let value = row.get(3)?; return Ok(KVRow{id,unixtime,key,value}); } } ``` Our error is evolving! (Plz dont sue me Nintendo). ``` mismatched types `anyhow::Error` and `rusqlite::Error` have similar names, but are actually distinct types. rustcClick for full compiler diagnostic lib.rs(394, 1): `anyhow::Error` is defined in crate `anyhow` error.rs(13, 1): `rusqlite::Error` is defined in crate `rusqlite` example.rs(69, 95): try wrapping the expression in `Ok`: `Ok(`, `)` ``` We can safely take the compilers advice here and just wrap KVRow::try_from(row) in Ok(T) but as for why that works ... In our try_from impl we specified that try_from returns an anyhow::Result, but F's definition says it returns a rusqlite::Result. If we wrap it in Ok, we'll get a rustqlite::Result\>, which is technically fine. ``` let value = conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| Ok(KVRow::try_from(row)))?; //let value: Result let value = conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| Ok(KVRow::try_from(row)))??; ``` Since theres nested results though we'd need two '?' operators. This is kinda sloppy though, and our desired return type is a Result\>. To get there we can use the OptionalExtension module of rusqlite. ``` Optional() Converts a Result into a Result>. By default, Rusqlite treats 0 rows being returned from a query that is expected to return 1 row as an error. This method will handle that error, and give you back an Option instead. ``` Slapping it on there (and accepting vscode's auto import request to import rusqlite::OptionalExt) yields this. ``` let value = conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| Ok(KVRow::try_from(row))).optional()?; //let value: Option> ``` Not quite there, we want that inner result to be collapsed. Backtracking a bit, we're going to use expect to unwrap the inner result from try_from. This will cause the program to panic! or basically crash at runtime if it fails to parse a row from storage into a KVRow, while providing an error message of our specification. In general, Results are best when you want to make use of conditional behavior at runtime based on whether something succeeds, whereas panics are appropriate if the error shouldn't be recovered from or ignorable. So we've finally arrived at our finished db_get()!. ``` rust fn db_get(conn: Connection, key: &str) -> Result> { // if the key is too big it cant be in the database if key.len() > 10_000 { return Ok(None); } let value = conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| Ok(KVRow::try_from(row).expect("Row did not match table schema"))) .optional()?; //let value: Option return Ok(value); } ``` And the compiler is content. But I'm not, lets go over testing before we move on to HTTP request handlers. # Testing We're going to start by moving over our database interactions to a shared library. Its really easy, just 2 steps. 1. make src/lib.rs 2. add this to your cargo.toml ``` toml [lib] name = "lib" path = "src/lib.rs" ``` 3. Cut and paste everything other than fn main()\{...}, as well as the necessary imports (the last 2) over to lib.rs. For clarity's sake : ``` rust use anyhow::{Result, ensure}; use rusqlite::{self, Connection, OptionalExtension, Row}; async fn init_db() -> Result { let conn = Connection::open_in_memory()?; //define schema conn.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS Storage (id : INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, unixtime: INTEGER, key : UNIQUE TEXT, value : TEXT); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_storage_keys ON storage (key); ", ())?; return Ok(conn); } enum SetError { Oversized, KeyAlreadyPresent, DBError(rusqlite::Error) } fn db_set(conn : &Connection, key : &str, val : &str) -> Result<() , SetError> { if key.len() > 10_000 || val.len() > 10_000 { return Err(SetError::Oversized) }; conn.execute("INSERT INTO storage (unixepoch(),$1,$2);", (key, val)) .map_err( |e| { match e.sqlite_error_code() { Some(rusqlite::ErrorCode::ConstraintViolation) => SetError::KeyAlreadyPresent, _ => SetError::DBError(e), } })?; return Ok(()); } #[derive(Debug)] struct KVRow { id : i64, unixtime: i64, key : String, value: String, } impl TryFrom<&Row<'_>> for KVRow { type Error = anyhow::Error; fn try_from(row: &Row) -> std::result::Result { let id = row.get(0)?; let unixtime = row.get(1)?; let key = row.get(2)?; let value = row.get(3)?; return Ok(KVRow{id,unixtime,key,value}); } } fn db_get(conn: Connection, key: &str) -> Result> { // if the key is too big it cant be in the database if key.len() > 10_000 { return Ok(None); } let value = conn.query_one("SELECT * FROM storage WHERE key = $1 LIMIT 1;", (key,), |row| Ok(KVRow::try_from(row).expect("Row did not match table schema"))) .optional()?; return Ok(value); } ``` 4. Mark anything we want to use outside this file as pub. So init_db, SetError, db_set, KVRow and all its members, and db_get. And there we go, we have a library. To test it, we have to use rust's nightly toolchain. I use it by default, but you can configure it on a per project basis by adding a rust-toolchain.toml file to your project's root, and writing this inside : ``` rust [toolchain] channel = "nightly" ``` 5. Copy this and append it to lib.rs. I have this snippet saved in my computer's projects folder for convenience. ```rust #[cfg(test)] mod test { use super::*; extern crate test; use test::Bencher; #[test] fn tester() { } #[bench] fn bencher(b : &mut Bencher) { b.iter(||{ }); } } ``` 6. Add #![feature(test)] to the top of lib.rs . You may need to restart the rust-analyzer extension. And now we can write tests and run them with a click of the mouse, or using the console! ``` rust #[test] async fn initialization_t() {/*errors*/} ``` Or not?! Turns out we just need to import a different test macro from the one in the standard library via tokio to test async functions. Lets write a simple one first - ``` rust async fn initialization_t() { let cnxn = init_db().await.unwrap(); cnxn.close().unwrap(); return; } ``` ``` thread 'test::initialization_t' panicked at src/lib.rs:75:36: called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: unrecognized token: ":" in CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS Storage (id : INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, unixtime: INTEGER, key : UNIQUE TEXT, value : TEXT); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_storage_keys ON storage (key); at offset 39 ``` And we're already learning ! I messed up and carried my rust syntax over into the sql of init db. No colons! There were some more errors after this and I got a bit stumped so I asked copilot. As it turns out, unique columns automatically get unique indexes, so attempting to create a unique index on them will always result in a constraint violation! So I removed the index and the test passed. Our new init_db is ``` rust pub async fn init_db() -> Result { let conn = Connection::open_in_memory()?; //define schema conn.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS storage (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, unixtime INTEGER, key TEXT UNIQUE, value TEXT);", ())?; return Ok(conn); } ``` Now lets test the setting and retrieval of data from our database. ``` rust #[tokio::test] async fn set_get_t() { let cnxn = init_db().await.unwrap(); //just add "#[derive(Debug)]" above SetError db_set(&cnxn, "hi", "bye").unwrap(); //forgot to take connection by reference! Should be db_get(conn: &Connection, println!("{:?}", db_get(&cnxn, "hi")); } ``` Once again, sql yields unhelpful error messages so we resort to copilot to learn that I've flubbed the sql syntax. It should be ``` conn.execute("INSERT INTO storage (unixtime, key, value) VALUES (unixepoch(),$1,$2);", (key, val))... ``` # Micro-Benchmarking Rusqlite I've kept the vestigial async bits around largely as a demonstration but for the purpose of benchmarking things theyre in the way, so I removed the tokio import and all the async and await bits. ```rust #[bench] fn bencher(b : &mut Bencher) { let cnxn = init_db().unwrap(); let mut start = 0; b.iter(|| { for keyi in start+0..start+1000 { let keys = keyi.to_string(); db_set(&cnxn, &keys, &keys).unwrap(); } for keyi in start+0..start+1000 { let keys = keyi.to_string(); db_get(&cnxn, &keys).unwrap(); } start += 1000; }); } ``` The code being benchmarked here is just the stuff inside the closure passed to bencher.iter( || \{\/\*stuff\*\/} ); I want to confirm that the unique keyword actually improves performance by indexing the table as expected. Never trust a clanker, afterall. But sure enough that seems to be the case! ``` bash running 1 test test test::bencher ... bench: 5,240,167.10 ns/iter (+/- 102,186.70) ``` Dumping and retrieving 1000 items takes 5ms with the unique keyword, and well, I have no idea how long it takes without it because it's been 5 minutes and the benchmark is still running, so I'm going to just kill it. Safe to say it works as intended. # Routing With Axum - Handlers It's time to set up our routes and start serving up some http responses! Back to main.rs. ``` rust //update to import debug_handler and response::IntoResponse use axum::{self, debug_handler, response::IntoResponse, routing::{delete, get, post, put}}; ... #[debug_handler] async fn hello() -> impl IntoResponse { return "hello"; } ``` Lets explain this a bit. 1. debug_handler is a macro that axum provides which provides more useful error messages for handler functions. 2. 'impl IntoResponse' is similar to a generic - it allows the function to return 1 concrete type that implements the IntoResponse trait. IntoResponse is a trait provided by axum and implemented for many common types, including tuples and axum native response types like JSON. The closure within get() on line 10 can now be replaced with the hello function. Now with the tutorial out of the way, lets make a put route that calls db_set and a get route for db_get. # Formatting Our imports are getting pretty ugly so I may as well cover formatting. Run cargo fmt in your project root to format all of your code in rust's default style. If you want to configure it, you can make a .rustfmt.toml file. Here's mine, customize it as you desire. ``` toml brace_style = "AlwaysNextLine" control_brace_style = "AlwaysNextLine" reorder_imports = true max_width = 110 struct_field_align_threshold = 32 imports_indent = "Visual" indent_style = "Visual" use_small_heuristics = "Default" ``` # Sharing State We don't have a global dynamic state like javascript or python, so to share our database connection between routes we need to use axum::extract::State. ``` rust let db = init_db().expect("Couldn't connect to database"); //let state = State(Arc::new(Mutex::new(db))); let api_router = axum::Router::new().route("/hello", get(hello)) .with_state(db) .into_make_service(); ``` Which yields the error : ``` rust the trait bound `rusqlite::Connection: Clone` is not satisfied the trait `Clone` is not implemented for `rusqlite::Connection` `RefCell` cannot be shared between threads safely ``` Some database connections will be cloneable, some not, depending on if they do synchronization internally. Rusqlite does not, and the RefCell means that we can't send references to it between threads either without a proper synchronization mechanism like Mutex or RWLock as well as an Atomic Reference-Counted Cell to ensure it lives long enough. ``` rust use std::{net::SocketAddr, sync::Arc}; use tokio::{self, sync::Mutex}; ... //main() let state = State(Arc::new(Mutex::new(db))); let api_router = axum::Router::new().route("/hello", get(hello)) .with_state(state) ``` IMPORTANT NOTE : when using async, be sure to use tokio::sync 's version of mutex, rwlock, channel, etc. over std::sync, to prevent blocking worker threads or potentially causing deadlocks. For now the compiler is happy but there's still problems with this we'll have to deal with in a moment. Lets change focus to getting data from the client into our handler function. Axum uses extractors for this. I want to use JSON for the api so lets take a look at the JSON extractor documentation.
" ⚠️ Since parsing JSON requires consuming the request body, the Json extractor must be last if there are multiple extractors in a handler. See “the order of extractors ”
Read more [here](https://docs.rs/axum/latest/axum/struct.Json.html). Mimicking the code in their examples we need to add a dependency on serde ``` bash cargo add serde --features derive ``` "use" it, and create a KVRequest struct that derives serde::Deserialize. ``` rust use serde::Deserialize; ... #[derive(Deserialize)] struct KVRequest {key : String, value: String} ``` Now we can write our handler function header like so ``` rust type AppState = State>>; ... async fn kv_set(state : AppState, req : Json) -> impl IntoResponse {return ();} ``` Except not! This is actually a strange error... ``` required by a bound in `__axum_macros_check_kv_set_0_from_request_check`rustcE0277 example.rs(33, 25): original diagnostic the trait bound `Arc>: FromRef<()>` is not satisfied ``` Its complaining that we cant build our entire app state from a reference to an empty tuple. Such a trait bound existing makes no sense. It turns out its originating from a bug in the debug_handler macro. It expects the first argument to be something like State\ so AppState seems to be messing with it. It turns out we don't need to wrap our appstate in state so we can just remove that. ```rust type AppState = Arc>; // in main() let state = Arc::new(Mutex::new(db)); ... async fn kv_set(state : State, req : Json) -> impl IntoResponse { let cnxn = state.lock().await; db_set(&cnxn, &req.key, &req.value).map_err(|e| anyhow!(e))?; return Ok(()); } ``` That should fix it but we have a new error now. ``` the method `anyhow_kind` exists for reference `&lib::SetError`, but its trait bounds were not satisfied the following trait bounds were not satisfied: `lib::SetError: Into` which is required by `lib::SetError: anyhow::kind::TraitKind` `lib::SetError: std::fmt::Display` ``` This one is a can we kicked down the road for brevity earlier when we defined SetError. Any decent error type needs to be convertible to a string. In rust, to_string() is provided by the Display trait. Anyhow expects Display to exist on anything we're giving it as an error for that purpose but we never implemented Display on SetError. ``` rust use std::fmt::Display; ... impl Display for SetError { fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result { write!(f, "{:?}", self) } } ``` This is the laziest possible implementation of Display - it actually constitutes a vulnerability in this use case, because we'll be sending our full error messages to the client which is not good. Proper practice is to send those to the console and just give the client what it needs to know. ``` rust impl Display for SetError { fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result { match self { Self::Oversized => write!(f, "Error: Oversized Key Or Value"), Self::KeyAlreadyPresent => write!(f, "Error: Key Already Present"), Self::DBError(e) => {println!("{:?}", e); write!(f, "Internal Server Error")} } } } ``` Thats better, but the 'proper'est way would probably be to use the tracing library. Axum's github has plenty of examples integrating it, and its included by default in their tutorial. ``` rust #[derive(Deserialize)] struct KVSetRequest {key : String, value: String} #[debug_handler] async fn kv_set(state : State, req : Json) -> Result { let cnxn = state.lock().await; db_set(&cnxn, &req.key, &req.value).map_err(|e| e.to_string())?; return Ok(()); } ``` We dont actually need the anyhow! macro anymore, as String implements IntoResponse directly. Now that we have set done, get is trivial - mostly a copy paste. ``` rust #[derive(Deserialize)] struct KVGetRequest {key : String} #[debug_handler] async fn kv_get(state: State, req: Json) -> Result { let cnxn = state.lock().await; let entry = db_get(&cnxn, &req.key).map_err(|e| e.to_string())?; return Ok(Json(entry)); } ``` And with that the server side is fully functional!
